title: Stochastic & Mini-Batch Gradient Descent for Deep Nets
concept: Deep networks train on huge datasets with millions of weights, so computing one exact gradient using the ENTIRE dataset before every update (full-batch) is too slow and memory-heavy — deep learning training loops rely on stochastic and mini-batch gradient descent instead.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes a deep network's loss landscape especially tricky compared to linear regression's smooth bowl?

options.text: It's non-convex — full of saddle points, flat plateaus, and many local minima across millions of dimensions
It only has one direction to search in
It's always a perfectly smooth, single dip

exercises.explain: Exactly! And finding a good low point in that messy terrain takes a LOT of steps. So how do we make each step affordable to compute?

cards.title: A dataset too big to hold
cards.body: Imagine training a deep network on 10 million images. Computing the exact gradient means checking EVERY image before taking just one tiny step. That's brutally slow — and sometimes it won't even fit in memory.

cards.title: Epochs, batches, and iterations
cards.body: One full pass through the entire training dataset is an epoch. The dataset gets split into mini-batches; each weight update from one mini-batch is an iteration. True SGD uses a batch of 1, but in deep learning 'SGD' is often used loosely to mean mini-batch training.

cards.title: Packing a backpack, not moving the whole house
cards.body: Full-batch gradient descent is like moving every single belonging out of your house before taking one step forward. Mini-batch training is like packing a backpack with a chunk of your things and adjusting your route as you go.

cards.title: Why GPUs love mini-batches
cards.body: GPU memory can only hold so much at once. A network with millions of weights plus a batch of 10 million images would blow past available memory — but a mini-batch of 32 to 256 examples fits comfortably, and a GPU crunches the whole batch in parallel almost as fast as a single example.

cards.title: More data per step isn't always better
cards.body: You might think bigger batches (closer to full-batch) always train faster since the gradient estimate is less noisy. But in deep nets, bigger batches need more memory and are slower per step — and the extra noise from smaller batches actually helps the optimizer escape saddle points and plateaus.

cards.title: Full-batch vs. mini-batch for deep nets
cards.body: How the two approaches handle a huge dataset:

left.text:
Loads the entire dataset
One slow step per epoch — often impossible

right.text:
Loads a small chunk
Many fast, GPU-friendly steps per epoch

exercises.prompt: Why is full-batch gradient descent often impractical for training a deep network on a huge dataset?

options.text: It requires computing the gradient over the whole dataset before every update — far too slow and memory-heavy
It always produces a worse-quality model than any other method
It's actually the fastest possible way to train, but nobody uses it

exercises.explain: Right! Massive datasets and millions of weights make one full-batch step painfully slow and memory-hungry.

exercises.prompt: True or false: one full pass through the entire training dataset is called an 'iteration.'
exercises.explain: False! A full pass through the dataset is an epoch. An iteration is a single weight update from one mini-batch.

exercises.sentence: Splitting a huge dataset into small chunks so a deep network can update its weights many times per epoch is called ___-batch gradient descent.
exercises.explain: Mini-batch gradient descent is deep learning's standard: small chunks, frequent updates, GPU-friendly.

exercises.prompt: A teammate says: 'We process the dataset in small random chunks of 64 examples, updating the weights after each chunk, so we get many updates per epoch.' What are they describing?

options.text: No training is happening at all

exercises.explain: Right — small chunks (batches) with frequent updates is exactly mini-batch training, the deep learning standard.

exercises.prompt: You're training a deep network on 5 million images with limited GPU memory. Batch size 1 makes training painfully noisy and slow to converge; loading all 5 million images before one update crashes your GPU from running out of memory. What's the practical fix, and why?

options.text: Use mini-batch gradient descent — a batch of a few dozen to a few hundred images balances GPU memory limits with steady, efficient progress
Switch to full-batch gradient descent, since more data per step is always strictly better
Train on only 10 images total to avoid memory issues
Batch size doesn't matter for GPU memory at all

exercises.explain: Exactly! Mini-batches are deep learning's goldilocks zone — GPU-friendly, memory-safe, and frequent enough to make real progress each epoch. But even with the right batch size, plain SGD's steps can still go wrong once they hit tricky terrain — that's next.
